chain_name: High acidity → antimicrobial and antioxidant protection → extended ageability

starting_factor.description: A wine with high total acidity and low pH has enhanced chemical and microbiological stability that underpins long-term ageing potential.

intermediate_steps.description: Low pH (high acidity) inhibits the growth of spoilage bacteria and wild yeasts that cause off-flavours in wine over time. Lactic acid bacteria (responsible for MLF and potential spoilage via Brett) are significantly inhibited below pH 3.5. This protection is fundamental to long-term bottle stability.

At low pH, SO₂ exists in a higher proportion as molecular SO₂ (the antimicrobial and antioxidant form) rather than dissociated bisulfite/sulfite ions. This means a lower total SO₂ addition achieves the same protective effect. High-acid wines are thus better protected against oxidation for a given SO₂ dose.

Acidity preserves fresh, primary and secondary aromas by slowing ester hydrolysis and oxidative degradation. This allows a wine to retain its aromatic complexity over decades rather than losing it in years. High-acid wines age into tertiary complexity while retaining structural freshness.

final_outcome.description: High acidity is one of the three pillars of ageability (alongside tannin for reds and sugar for sweet wines). It functions as a preservative, an SO₂ potentiator, a microbial inhibitor, and an aromatic stabiliser — all simultaneously.

examples.description: The combination of high residual sugar, very high acidity, and low alcohol enables Mosel Riesling to age for decades, developing petrol/kerosene notes (TDN — 1,1,6-trimethyl-1,2-dihydronaphthalene) and honeyed complexity while retaining remarkable freshness.

Low-alcohol, bone-dry, searingly acidic Hunter Valley Sémillon transforms from a austere, reductive young wine into a toasty, honeyed, complex aged wine — driven entirely by acidity-enabled slow oxidative evolution.

related_exam_questions: Explain how acidity contributes to the ageing potential of wine.
Why does a high-acid wine require less SO₂ for protection than a low-acid wine?
What three structural components are most associated with long ageing potential in wine?
distinction_note: A Distinction answer goes beyond 'acidity = freshness' to explain the microbial inhibition mechanism, the pH/SO₂ molecular form relationship, and the specific role in aroma preservation — while naming at least one concrete high-acid aged wine style.
